perturbation.original_text: LEADER hereby agrees to indemnify and hold EZJR, it successors, and assigns harmless from and against any and all damage or deficiency resulting from any material misrepresentation, breach of warranty or covenant, or nonfulfillment of any agreement on the part of LEADER under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: LEADER agrees to indemnify EZJR from damage directly caused by gross negligence, not exceeding the total compensation paid to LEADER under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change significantly limits the scope of indemnification, potentially contradicting Nevada's requirements for fair and reasonable contractual terms and good faith. It removes protection for EZJR from damages resulting from misrepresentation, breach of warranty or covenant, or nonfulfillment of the agreement. By limiting the indemnity to gross negligence and capping the amount, it creates a situation where EZJR might not be fully compensated for damages suffered due to LEADER's actions, if those actions fall short of gross negligence, or where the damages exceed the compensation amount.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Nevada Revised Statutes (NRS) Chapter 104 - Uniform Commercial Code (specifically provisions relating to good faith and fair dealing)
perturbation.law_explanation: NRS 104.1304 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of contracts. By narrowly limiting the scope and amount of indemnification, the modified text potentially violates this obligation if it shields LEADER from liability for breaches of contract or misrepresentations that cause significant damage to EZJR, effectively undermining the spirit of the agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly reduces the scope of indemnification, which contradicts the obligation of good faith in contract performance as highlighted in Scraped Law Snippet 1: "Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement." By limiting indemnity to gross negligence and capping the amount, the changed text weakens EZJR's protection against losses from LEADER's actions, potentially shielding LEADER from liability for breaches or misrepresentations, thus violating the implied duty of good faith by undermining the original agreement's intent to provide comprehensive protection.

perturbation.original_text: If any action is brought among the parties with respect to this Agreement or otherwise, by way of a claim or counterclaim, the parties agree that in any such action, and on all issues, the parties irrevocably waive their right to a trial by jury.
perturbation.changed_text: If any action is brought among the parties with respect to this Agreement or otherwise, by way of a claim or counterclaim, the parties agree that in any such action, the parties waive their right to a trial by jury.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the word 'irrevocably' from the jury waiver clause. Under Nevada law, jury waivers must be knowing and voluntary. Removing 'irrevocably' could be interpreted as weakening the certainty and permanence of the waiver, thus potentially making it easier to challenge the waiver's validity in court, or implying the waiver can be changed later. This creates legal uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 1, Section 3 of the Nevada Constitution guarantees the right to a jury trial. While this right can be waived, the waiver must be done knowingly and voluntarily. By removing 'irrevocably', the clause becomes ambiguous about the firmness of the waiver, creating a potential for a court to find the waiver unenforceable because it does not clearly demonstrate a knowing and voluntary relinquishment of the constitutional right to a jury trial.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: THE CONSTITUTION OF THE STATE OF NEVADA [Rev. 12/9/2024 3:04:59 PM--2024R1] THE CONSTITUTION OF THE STATE OF NEVADA _________ [The Nevada constitution was framed by a convention of delegates chosen by the people. The convention met at Carson City on July 4, 1864, and adjourned on July 28 of the same year. On the 1st Wednesday of September 1864, the constitution was approved by the vote of the people of the Territory of Nevada, and on October 31, 1864, President Lincoln proclaimed that the State of Nevada was admitted into the Union on an equal footing with the original states. The literal text of the original, signed copy of the constitution filed in the office of the secretary of state has been retained, unless it has been repealed or superseded by amendment. Where the original text has been amended or where a new provision has been added to the original constitution, the source of the amendment or addition is indicated in the source note immediately following the text of the amended or new section. Leadlines for sections have been supplied by the Legislative Counsel of the State of Nevada.] _________ [Preliminary Action.] Ordinance. Preamble. Article. 1 . Declaration of Rights. 2 . Right of Suffrage. 3 . Distribution of Powers. 4 . Legislative Department. 5 . Executive Department. 6 . Judicial Department. 7 . Impeachment and Removal From Office. 8 . Municipal and Other Corporations. 9 . Finance and State Debt. 10 . Taxation. 11 . Education. 12 . Militia. 13 . Public Institutions. 14 . Boundary. 15 . Miscellaneous Provisions. 16 . Amendments. 17 . Schedule. XVIII . [Right of Suffrage.] Repealed in 1992. 19 . Initiative and Referendum. [Election Ordinance.] _________ [PRELIMINARY ACTION.] Whereas, The Act of Congress Approved March Twenty First A.D. Eighteen Hundred and Sixty Four To enable the People of the Territory of Nevada to form a Constitution and State Government and for the admission of such State into the Union on an equal footing with the Original States, requires that the Members of the Convention for framing said Constitution shall, after Organization, on behalf of the people of said Territory, adopt the Constitution of the United States.—Therefore, Be it Resolved, That the Members of this Convention, elected by the Authority of the aforesaid enabling Act of Congress, Assembled in Carson City the Capital of said Territory of Nevada, and immediately subsequent to its Organization, do adopt, on behalf of the people of said Territory the Constitution of the United States[.] ORDINANCE Slavery prohibited; freedom of religious worship; disclaimer of public lands. [Effective through November 25, 2024, and after that date until the date Congress consents to the amendment proposed by Senate Joint Resolution No. 27 (1993) or a legal determination is made that such consent is not necessary, if: (1) the provisions of Assembly Joint Resolution No. 10 (2021) are not approved and ratified by the voters at the 2024 General Election; and (2) before November 26, 2024, Congress does not consent to the amendment proposed by Senate Joint Resolution No. 27 (1993) or a legal determination is not made that such consent is no
perturbation.justification: The removal of 'irrevocably' weakens the jury waiver clause. The Law Explanation states that waivers must be done knowingly and voluntarily, and removing 'irrevocably' makes the firmness of the waiver ambiguous. Scraped Law Snippet 2 refers to the Nevada Constitution, which guarantees certain rights, including the right to a jury trial (Article 1). Although the snippet itself doesn't explicitly detail the requirements for a valid waiver, the Law Explanation fills in the gap, indicating that waivers must be knowing and voluntary. The Perturbation Explanation supports this by stating that the removal could imply the waiver is not permanent, creating legal uncertainty and potentially making it easier to challenge. Because the change weakens the assurance of a 'knowing and voluntary' waiver, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: All notices, requests, demands, and other communications required or permitted hereunder will be in writing and will be deemed to have been duly given when delivered by hand, by overnight courier, or fax, or two days after being mailed by certified or registered mail, return receipt requested, with postage prepaid.
perturbation.changed_text: All notices, requests, demands, and other communications required or permitted hereunder will be in writing and will be deemed to have been duly given when delivered by hand or by mail.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes specific methods of delivery (overnight courier, fax, certified/registered mail with return receipt). Nevada law requires reasonable notification methods in contractual relationships. Removing these methods increases the risk that notices will not be properly received or documented, potentially leading to disputes and legal challenges.
perturbation.law_explanation: NRS 104.1202(e) states that a person notifies or gives a notice or notification to another person by taking such steps as may be reasonably required to inform the other person in ordinary course, whether or not such other person actually comes to know of it. The original clause describes means that ensure the notification is given in an ordinary and reasonable manner, while the edited version opens for less reliable notification options such as an ordinary mail, which may be argued to be not as reliable as other options. This could be interpreted as not fulfilling the requirement of reasonably informing the other party.

perturbation.justification: The modification removes specific notification methods (overnight courier, fax, certified/registered mail with return receipt) that ensure reliable delivery and documentation. The Law Explanation states that the original clause ensures notification is given in an ordinary and reasonable manner, and the changed version opens for less reliable notification options, such as ordinary mail, which may be argued to be not as reliable as other options. NRS 104.1202(e), as referenced in the Law Explanation, states that a person notifies another person by taking such steps as may be reasonably required to inform the other person in ordinary course. Removing reliable methods contradicts the requirement of reasonably informing the other party, creating a legal contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 includes NRS 104.1202, which supports this interpretation.
